data.title: Astera Labs: Buy The Trade War Pullback, Strong Buy
data.description:
Authored by Garvit Bhandari

Recap of previous article on ALAB

The previous article on Astera Labs, Inc. (NASDAQ:ALAB) highlighted the attractiveness of its product portfolio, strong demand from hyperscalers and its upbeat growth prospects given a large and rapidly expanding TAM.

In this follow-up article, I am highlighting a promising 2025 driven by revenue contribution from all four product lines. The company is well positioned for the next phase of growth. I continue to remain bullish and reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

Investment Summary

Astera Labs is a leading provider of semiconductor-based connectivity solutions for cloud and AI infrastructure.

Thanks to its significant investments in the business during 2024, ALAB is well positioned to capitalize on the large market opportunities that lies ahead.

2025 will see revenue contribution from all four product lines and the growth prospects remain bright. The stock has given up all the gains of 2024, and I believe the significant correction offers a compelling entry point for long-term investors. I have a Strong Buy rating and a target price of $124.

Next phase of growth driven by all four product lines

ALAB delivered strong results for FY24. Full-year revenues stood at $396.3 million, a YoY jump of 242%. Moreover, during the year, the company invested significantly in its R&D activities. The overall headcount increased by around 80% to 440 full-time employees.

I strongly believe that ALAB's investments are likely to pay off in 2025, driven by contribution from all of its four product lines. Because, across the industry, hyperscalers are demanding better products that support large language models which continue to grow in complexity and capacity. There is also the fact that hyperscalers have massive capex investments lined up in cloud and AI infrastructure. For instance, ALAB has developed customized AI racks based on third-party GPUs that will be used in datacenters, with its Aries and Scorpio products to maximize GPU throughput and with volume deployments expected in the second half of the year.

In regard to ALAB's product portfolio, I expect its Aries and Taurus product lines to deliver on their strong growth trajectory in FY25. Meanwhile, its Leo product line is likely to witness volume ramp up in the second half of FY25. However, I'm especially enthusiastic about ALAB's Scorpio switches. These switches will begin ramping during the year with new engagements. Given the large market opportunity, Scorpio switches are likely to become the largest of the product lines.

Significant stock price correction provides a compelling entry point

ALAB had seen a stellar run-up during 2024 but has since corrected significantly, giving away all the gains. I believe this significant correction offers a compelling entry opportunity for long-term investors.

ALAB was listed in March 2024 and rose handsomely by ~72% to close at ~$62 on its first trading day. Given its promising AI and cloud applications, the stock zoomed to around ~$147 in December 2024.

However, the stock has given up all its gains in YTD 2025 driven by three reasons: the impact of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) on AI stocks, its 4Q24 earnings and recent tariff concerns. The stock is currently trading at ~$53 , which is lower than its IPO debut. I believe this significant correction provides a compelling entry point.

Guidance

ALAB has issued the following guidance for 1Q25:

  • Revenues in the range of $151 to $155 million.
  • Non-GAAP gross margins of 74%.
  • Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $0.28 to $0.29.

Financial Projections

ALAB delivered an impressive YoY growth of 242% for FY24. Based on its 1Q25 topline guidance of $151-$155 million, I am modelling in around 70% growth for FY25. Moreover, I expect around 30% topline growth during FY26-27 as newer product lines contribute to the overall revenue.

Based on the revenue growth, I expect EBITDA margins to improve from 31% in FY24 to 37% for FY27. On similar lines, I am estimating non-GAAP EPS of $1.43 for FY25 and $2.41 for FY27.

Valuation

For valuation purposes, I'm using a combination of P/S and P/E to arrive at the fair value of ALAB.

I have compared ALAB with its peers for each of its product lines. I value ALAB using target multiples of 18x sales for the P/S method and 50x earnings using P/E. These target multiples are at a significant premium to its peer group. I attribute this premium to its superior revenue growth, ~50% over FY24-26e vs. 23% for the peer group.

The average target price (TP) stands at $124 based on $126 for P/S and $121 using P/E.

Risks

Below are the main risks to my investment thesis:

  • The company has a history of reporting net losses, and it is not clear when it will report profitability.
  • ALAB derives a substantial part of its revenue from a few customers (3 customers account for 71% of total revenue for FY24). As such, there is a customer concentration risk and any loss of any of these customers will have an adverse impact on the company's business.
  • The company has a limited operating history and may not sustain its high growth rate.

Conclusion

ALAB is a leading provider of semiconductor-based connectivity solutions for cloud and AI infrastructure. Over the last year, the company has made significant investments in the business and has seen 80% growth in its employee base. These investments are expected to pay off in 2025 with all four product lines contributing to the topline.

The stock has given up all its gains of 2024, and I believe the correction provides a compelling entry point for long-term investors. I have a Strong Buy rating and a target price of $124.

data.title: MongoDB: Temporary Weakness Has Created An Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
MongoDB's (NASDAQ:MDB) share price has been under pressure in recent months, with investors likely concerned about the company's lack of direct exposure to AI, competition from the likes of PostgreSQL and general macro uncertainty. MongoDB continues to land customers at a healthy pace, though, and AI-driven infrastructure modernization and AI-enabled app modernization could provide growth tailwinds, particularly if the demand environment improves.

I previously suggested that the recent pullback was a buying opportunity. While MongoDB's share price is down around 30% since then, my opinion hasn't changed. MongoDB is a high-quality company operating under challenging conditions. Even if MongoDB remains a somewhat niche solution, there is still potential for double-digit revenue growth over the long-term and likely high margins at scale, which should lead to strong returns.

Market Conditions

MongoDB's business continues to underperform relative to what might be expected based on AWS' growth. This is unsurprising though given MongoDB's lack of direct exposure to AI.

It should also be kept in mind that consumption is tied to the applications built on MongoDB's platform, meaning its business has relatively high exposure to economic conditions. MongoDB's customer base also skews towards SMBs, who have been hard hit by economic conditions in recent years.

Competition from both the hyperscalers and PostgreSQL remain concerns for MongoDB. Adoption of PostgreSQL appears to be fairly rapid at the moment, which MongoDB is attributing to the ease of shifting from Oracle, Sybase, and SQL Server. I question the extent to which MongoDB is competing directly with PostgreSQL, though. Regardless, MongoDB has suggested its win rates against PostgreSQL are high, as are its win rates against the hyperscalers.

The number of job openings mentioning MongoDB in the job requirements points towards stabilization or even a return to growth, rather than ongoing weakness and competitive pressure. While this is a noisy indicator, it aligns with the growth of MongoDB's customer base and the company's commentary.

Even if MongoDB remains a relatively niche solution, I believe the company is well positioned due to the rapid growth of unstructured data. For example, an estimated 80% of the data generated in 2025 will be unstructured, and its growth is expected to significantly outpace that of structured data.

MongoDB Business Updates

While MongoDB expects AI to continue to proliferate, this will be a gradual process, and hence tailwinds in FY26 are likely to be modest. Many customers are still developing the necessary capabilities and there are still issues around things like hallucinations.

MongoDB is trying to help address hallucination concerns with its recent acquisition of Voyage AI. The acquisition provides embedding and reranking models which help to ensure accurate and relevant results. Total consideration was 220 million USD, with 20 million USD of this in cash. Voyage AI will add an immaterial amount of revenue in FY26.

Another focus area for MongoDB is using AI to support application modernization. MongoDB has demonstrated that its services and AI tooling can significantly accelerate modernization efforts and reduce cost. MongoDB is hoping to expand these efforts this year, potentially leading to stronger growth in FY27. This could provide a service revenue boost in the short term, but this work is likely to be performed at a loss. The company will initially target Java apps running on Oracle.

MongoDB also believes that it will benefit from AI applications using its database, although usage of these applications will need to scale first. Thousands of small customers are building AI apps on MongoDB, and several large AI companies are also using MongoDB. Some of these AI applications are reportedly gaining traction, although the revenue likely isn't meaningful for MongoDB yet. Customers are also adopting MongoDB's vector database.

Financial Analysis

MongoDB generated 548 million USD in the fourth quarter, up 20% YoY. Atlas revenue increased 24%, with better than anticipated consumption. Non-Atlas deals were also unexpectedly strong in the quarter, although this strength isn't really indicative of the current state of that part of the business. MongoDB has stated that its non-Atlas ARR growth was in the mid-single digits in Q4, down from low-double digit growth in the prior year comparable period. The decline in growth is likely due to EA customers deploying incremental workloads on Atlas.

MongoDB expects 524-529 million USD revenue in Q1, representing roughly 17% YoY growth at the midpoint. With a typical earnings beat, I believe growth is likely to come in closer to 20%, though. For the full year, growth is expected to be in the low teens, with the non-Atlas business declining as a result of fewer multi-year deals. Atlas consumption growth is expected to be stable in FY26 though, and guidance seems to suggest that Atlas growth will stabilize in the mid to low 20% range.

MongoDB now has 54,500 customers, adding 1,900 customers in the fourth quarter. This growth is fairly strong, given that MongoDB is now focusing on larger customers. Sales force productivity is reportedly improving, and MongoDB's win rates and retention rates are strong. MongoDB's net ARR expansion rate was approximately 118% in Q4, declining modestly sequentially. While this expansion rate is healthy, at some point newer solutions like search, vector search and stream processing will need to provide a boost.

MongoDB's non-GAAP operating income in Q4 was 112 million USD at a 21% margin. Operating cash flow totalled 50 million USD in Q4. CapEx was elevated due to the final purchase of IPv4 addresses, which amounted to 24 million USD.

Non-GAAP income from operations is expected to total 54-58 million USD in Q1. For the full year, non-GAAP income from operations is expected to be 210-230 million USD. This represents roughly a 5% margin decline from the prior year, which MongoDB has suggested will be due to an absence of high margin multi-year license revenue. MongoDB is also increasing its investments in R&D to improve scalability and performance and to add functionality to its database. In addition, the company is increasing its marketing investments to try and drive adoption of its solutions.

While MongoDB's margins are far from impressive at the moment, I believe the company can achieve operating margins of around 30% at maturity. This is largely the result of its relatively high retention rate. Margins of this magnitude lie many years in the future, though.

Conclusion

MongoDB's business has struggled in recent years, which, I believe, is largely the result of a challenging macro environment. While I don't expect this situation to change in the short-term, there are signs that MongoDB's business remains relatively healthy, despite growing concerns about competition. In particular, the success of PostgreSQL rightly raises questions about MongoDB's competitive positioning, although I tend to think it mainly indicates that NoSQL remains a niche solution.

Analysts are expecting MongoDB's revenue growth rate to stabilize in the mid to high teens, which I believe ignores both the headwinds the company has faced in recent years and the tailwinds that could emerge in coming years. MongoDB's expansion and retention rates remain healthy, and the company's customer base continues to grow at a solid pace, despite a shift in focus towards larger customers. The database category has historically been very attractive for market leaders, and MongoDB's margins should continue to improve as its business scales and matures.

While MongoDB has little direct exposure to AI, tailwinds could emerge over the next few years. For example, generative AI increases the value of unstructured data and could force companies to modernize their infrastructure. AI also lowers switching costs, something that MongoDB is leaning into with both products and services.

Even if MongoDB's growth rate doesn't rebound, the company appears attractively priced at the moment. I believe that MongoDB is capable of achieving operating margins in excess of 30% at maturity, which likely means that the company will have a PE ratio of around 13 within the next 5 years, using normalized earnings. MongoDB also has around 2.3 billion USD liquidity and no debt, positioning the company to return large amounts of capital to shareholders as margins move higher.

The company's revenue multiple is now also near all-time lows, although this must be weighed against the fact that growth has also decelerated sharply in recent years. Relative to comparable companies with similar growth rates, MongoDB generally has a much lower revenue multiple, though.

While MongoDB is a high-quality company that is reasonably priced, there are still substantial risks involved with an investment. MongoDB is only marginally profitable and continues to invest aggressively in future growth, creating a risk of larger losses in the event growth comes in weaker than expected. The demand environment could also deteriorate, which would likely drag MongoDB's growth and share price lower.

data.title: Aristotle Atlantic's Core Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

The U.S. equity market began 2025 with a modest decline, with the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) falling 4.27% during the first quarter. In contrast, bonds provided a measure of stability, as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

On a sector basis, negative performance was led by four of the eleven sectors within the S&P 500 Index in the first quarter of 2025. The weakest sectors were Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology. The best-performing sectors were Energy and Health Care.

U.S. economic data reported during the quarter presented a mixed picture. Real GDP growth slowed to an annualized rate of 2.4%, while inflation remained stable. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 2.8% year-over-year in February, reflecting moderate inflationary pressures. Meanwhile, the labor market remained resilient, with the unemployment rate hovering around 4%. However, consumer strength showed signs of strain, as retail sales slowed from levels seen late last year, potentially impacted by adverse weather and broader macroeconomic uncertainty.

Trade policy was a major source of uncertainty during the quarter. President Trump announced a series of new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico and China, citing concerns over illegal immigration, drug trafficking and intellectual property theft. Targeted industries included autos, steel, aluminum and energy, particularly Venezuelan oil. While tariffs initially raised concerns, the administration’s selective enforcement and flexible implementation approach helped ease market anxiety. In response to the evolving economic landscape, the Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25% to 4.50%. The central bank acknowledged potential inflationary pressures from tariffs and moderated expectations for economic growth in 2025.

Despite broader economic headwinds, corporate earnings remained strong. S&P 500 companies reported impressive 17.8% year-over-year earnings growth, the highest rate since 2021. However, tariff-related uncertainties loomed large, with more than 220 companies referencing tariffs in their earnings calls and nearly 15% issuing negative earnings guidance.

On the domestic front, a government shutdown was averted, as President Trump signed a six-month funding bill. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer supported the measure, believing that a shutdown would have allowed the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to terminate government services at a faster rate.

Geopolitically, the U.S. continued its mediation efforts in the Middle East and Ukraine. While a temporary ceasefire agreement was reached between Israel and Hamas in January, tensions flared again in March over disputes regarding hostage releases in Gaza. In Ukraine, U.S. aid was briefly paused following a contentious White House meeting between presidents Trump and Zelensky. Financial and intelligence support resumed after Ukraine signaled it was open to a ceasefire and agreed to revisit terms of a potential mineral deal, aiming to offset the costs of U.S. assistance.

Performance and Attribution Summary

For the first quarter of 2025, Aristotle Atlantic’s Core Equity Composite posted a total return of -5.90% gross of fees (-5.99% net of fees), underperforming the S&P 500 Index, which recorded a total return of -4.27%.

Performance (%) 1Q25 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception*
Core Equity Composite (gross) -5.90 6.76 7.59 17.19 12.58 13.41
Core Equity Composite (net) -5.99 6.32 7.15 16.72 12.10 12.90
S&P 500 Index -4.27 8.25 9.06 18.59 12.10 12.92

During the first quarter, the portfolio’s underperformance relative to the S&P 500 Index was due to a mix of security selection and allocation effects. Security selection in Information Technology and Industrials detracted the most from relative performance; conversely, security selection in Consumer Discretionary and Utilities contributed the most to relative performance.

Contributors and Detractors for 1Q 2025

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) Broadcom (AVGO)
Guardant Health (GH) ServiceNow (NOW)
O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY) Bio-Techne (TECH)
Antero Resources (AR) Oracle (ORCL)
Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) Chart Industries (GTLS)

Detractors

Broadcom

Broadcom detracted from performance in the first quarter. The release of the DeepSeek AI model and analysis of technological specifications stunned investors. This raised concerns about a rapid decline in AI model development costs and the effect that will have on capex spending for new infrastructure and advanced semiconductors used for accelerated computing needs. The entire AI infrastructure investment space saw significant declines in the final week of January following the DeepSeek announcements and continued to see further weakness in March as concerns around AI capex cuts increased.

ServiceNow

ServiceNow detracted from performance in the first quarter, as the company reported guidance during the fourth quarter earnings call that was below investor expectations. Investors have also begun to incorporate tougher macroeconomic headwinds and negative impacts from the DOGE government spending cuts into their weaker outlook for software revenue growth in 2025.

Contributors

Intercontinental Exchange

Intercontinental Exchange was a relative contributor in the first quarter following a solid fourth quarter earnings report highlighted by continued strong trading activity in energy and interest rate products, additional efficiency gains, and positive commentary about the Mortgage Technology business. In addition, optimism improved following the company’s annual ICE Experience conference, which highlighted new AI solutions in its Mortgage Technology product suite. The company’s AI efforts in Mortgage Technology are accelerating progress toward improving and digitizing workflows and positioning it to gain an increasing share of the long-term market opportunity in the mortgage industry.

Guardant Health

Guardant Health was a relative contributor in the first quarter following several positive announcements and solid fourth quarter earnings. Guardant announced Advanced Diagnostic Laboratory Test (ADLT) pricing from Medicare on its Guardant Shield test and a contract with the VA hospital system to cover patients over 45 years of age. This follows strong momentum and positive full-year revenue guidance.

Recent Portfolio Activity

The table below shows all buys and sells completed during the quarter, followed by a brief rationale.

Buys Sells
Analog Devices (ADI)

Buys

Analog Devices

Analog Devices is a global semiconductor leader dedicated to solving customers’ most complex engineering challenges. The company delivers innovations that connect technology to human breakthroughs and play a critical role at the intersection of the physical and digital worlds by providing the building blocks to sense, measure, interpret, connect and power. Analog designs, manufactures, tests and markets a broad portfolio of solutions. These solutions include integrated circuits, software and subsystems that leverage high-performance analog, mixed-signal and digital signal processing technologies. Its comprehensive product portfolio, deep domain expertise and advanced manufacturing span high-performance precision and high-speed mixed-signal, power management and processing technologies, including data converters, amplifiers, power management, radio frequency, integrated circuits, edge processors and other sensors. The company’s customers include original equipment manufacturers and customers that build electronic subsystems for integration into larger systems.

We see the company’s analog products providing exposure to high-growth trends, including automotive electrification and driver assistance systems, factory intelligence and automation, the Intelligent Edge, Internet of Things device proliferation and sustainable energy. We expect the company to return excess free cash flow, benefiting shareholders.

Sells

There were no sales in the first quarter of 2025.

Outlook

The equity markets in the first quarter declined as investors digested the potential impact of wide-ranging tariffs, along with concerns of a peak in the capital spending cycle around AI. Interest rates declined in the quarter, with the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield down about 35 basis points. The prospects of tariffs pushing prices higher could put the Fed on hold in a period when the economy starts to weaken. Economic data was mostly in line with expectations, reflecting a moderately growing economy. Although equity market valuations have pulled back, they are still in elevated territory with growing concerns of a slowdown in corporate profits. Our focus will continue to be at the company level, with an emphasis on seeking to invest in companies with secular tailwinds or strong product-driven cycles.

data.title: Apple: Proceed With Caution
data.description:
Let's get one thing straight from the get-go. I own Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA), which is one of my oldest holdings. Thanks to the long holding period, I managed to secure a solid average price per share and benefit from numerous shareholder rewards over the years. I don't feel the need to sell, but I'm not buying either (yet), as there's still high degree of uncertainty surrounding Apple.

With Trump, there are only two certainties - dynamic actions and uncertainty

Not even two weeks have passed since the U.S. flipped the global trading order by introducing substantial tariffs on most countries in the world. I assume most of you follow the subject closely, so let me just mention the current state. The trade war between the U.S. and the rest of the world changed (at least for some time) to war between the U.S. and China.

With tariffs ranging from 125% to 145% (depending on the side of the discussion), the highly positive news dropped on Saturday - hinting upcoming stock price increases (I'm writing before Monday's opening on March 14, 2025). The US exempted some electronics, including smartphones, from tariff policy:

US President Donald Trump's administration has exempted smartphones, computers and some other electronic devices from "reciprocal" tariffs, including the 125% levies imposed on Chinese imports.

In a notice, US Customs and Border Patrol said that the goods would be excluded from Trump's 10% global tariff on most countries and the much larger Chinese import tax.

It marks the first significant reprieve of any kind in Trump's tariffs on China, with one trade analyst describing it as a "game-changer scenario".

However, the joy will likely not last for long as Donald Trump doesn't intend to spare these items from tariffs policy, but address them with a different "box":

Donald Trump says Chinese-made smartphones and other electronics will not be exempt from tariffs - adding they are simply moving into a different levy "bucket".

European stock markets bounced up on Monday morning after Friday's official announcement that some of these products would escape levies of up to 145%.

(...)

However US officials said on Sunday that products would be subject to a "semiconductor tariff" instead, with Trump expected to reveal more details later.

Let me remind you, these news came up barely two days apart from each other. If that's not a chaotic approach to international negotiations and trade policies, I don't know what is. Therefore, regardless of what happens with the electronics segment, investors are likely to experience elevated volatility. Having seen what Trump's announcements made to global stock markets over the last (not even) two weeks, I've got no reason not to expect further (even rapid) stock market reactions - either up or down, depending on the approach to respective policies.

Fundamentals remain strong, but are they as strong as they used to be?

We have the data available up to the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal period, which corresponds to the Q4 2024 calendar period. I will use calendar periods to present the data coherently to the sales seasonality (it should help our analysis).

During the last five years, Apple generated over 8% net sales CAGR, which is quite impressive at first sight, but we have to remember that the majority of this growth was accomplished in 2020 when revenue increased by nearly 10% and in 2021 when revenue increased by over 28.5%. The 2023-2024 period wasn't that impressive, with a sales decline of 0.5% in 2023 and a modest increase by 2.6% in 2024.

Also, please note that we have our seasonality in check here, as we look at full calendar periods. Naturally, iPhone remains Apple's leading segment with over 50% share in revenue in 2024. Its releases (typically in Q4 of a respective year) drove sales growth during forth quarters and supported complementary sales of other items like iPad and accessories. While the sales trend-line is growing, the growth dynamic itself was quite modest during the last two years.

As you can see in the table below, historically, around one-third of annual revenue was generated in Q4 2024 with iPhone and Accessories driving year-end sales.

While current results may prove to be quite solid given the strong consumer demand anticipating price increases (I see a lot of traffic in Apple Stores), the "El Dorado" may end soon. Should Trump introduce his tariffs including items that could hurt Apple, prices will likely increase. Depending on the demand elasticity from the price standpoint (considering also the risk of economic downturn in the U.S., potentially further hurting consumers' pockets), Apple could face even slower growth or even sales declines.

What's the good news? Apple upholds strong profitability, outperforming its peers in the consumer hardware space, with an EBITDA margin exceeding 34%. That constitutes a solid safety pillow for harder times.

The bottom line: I'm holding and observing

My bottom line is simple - I'm holding on to my stake of shares. If any unfavorable policies come to life, Apple won't be the only one affected by it, and when it comes to the strength of its brand and consumers' attachment to it - I believe the Company will still come out on top.

Apple may face some headwinds in terms of revenue declines or margin contractions (should Trump really introduce such tariffs). If not, it will likely keep growing its revenue with a single-digit dynamic and gradually improve profitability.

The valuation is now much more attractive than when I covered it last time and stands at ~20.6x forward-looking EV-to-EBITDA, but it's still not the "buy" level for me, given the risks surrounding the Company and its market.

Therefore, Apple is a "hold" for me. Cheers!

data.title: IGSB: Low Equity Beta Means High Diversification Attributes
data.description:
The equity market has been wild recently, like a rollercoaster. Some technical analysts would suggest that this might foreshadow a big move either to the upside or the downside, in what would be called a "Breakout" or "Breakdown".

For me, it's clear, an era of free trade is at an end, and a trade war with China is going to cause a very large portion of the equity market to struggle, with productivity, volumes and margins. Some stocks will do better than others, and over the longer term, I suspect stocks aligned to important megatrends will still do well. If I was going to bet on a Breakout or a Breakdown, I would tend towards a market breakdown.

If that's what you are thinking too, then it's time for a strategic asset allocation shift, tilting your portfolio weights so that you have less equity and more fixed income. While, in most cases, it is not convenient to buy outright bonds in such a way to achieve sufficient diversification, there are a number of excellent and low cost fixed income ETF portfolios in the market.

This article will cover the iShares 1-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IGSB) which is a very defensive vehicle, and a great option for this time of market volatility. While gold has been performing well and is a generally a good inflation hedge and likely a good asset to hold in times of risk-off markets, the problem with gold is it has no yield, and so I don't believe gold warrants more than 5-10% of our portfolios.

If you are looking to hold a meaningful allocation, say something around 40% in a lower risk asset, it has to, in my opinion, have a yield. That's where investment grade bonds come in.

In the battle for investment survival, capital preservation is paramount.

IGSB Portfolio Characteristics

As the name suggests, IGSB holds corporate bonds with maturities of mainly one to five years. In fact, 54% of the portfolio is in bonds of 1–3 years and 43% are in bonds of 3-5 years maturity. Its effective duration, a measure of interest rate sensitivity, is just 2.6 years.

In terms of credit quality, the fund holds 9% in AA rated bonds, 46% in A rated bonds, and 44% in BBB rated bonds. This allows it to achieve an average yield to maturity of 4.98%. Compare that to 3 and 5 year US Treasuries yielding 3.98% and 4.16%, respectively. IGSB makes distributions monthly.

The fund has over 4,100 holdings, across U.S. investment grade corporate bonds with the top sector weightings being banking at 32%, consumer non-cyclical at 11%, consumer cyclical at 9% and technology at 7.5%.

As well as a low sensitivity to interest rates - the 2.6 years effective duration, the portfolio has a low equity market beta, with its 3-year equity beta at just 0.14. This effectively means the correlation with the S&P 500 is very low.

Like its large sister iShares ETFs, it has a very low expense ratio of just 0.04%. This achieved by the economies of scale that iShares can achieve and also because the fund is passively tracking the ICE Bank of America 1-5 Year Corporate Bond index.

Risks

Similar to all fixed income products, an inflationary environment would erode real returns. The 32% weighting to the banking index could be a risk. However, as Jamie Dimon said in his recent interview with Fox Business, the banking sector is well capitalised and a repeat of the global financial crisis of 2007-2008 is unlikely.

Another risk is reinvestment risk. Although a Fed cutting of interest rates would, assuming unchanged credit spreads, boost bond prices in the short term, as this ETF invests 28% of the fund in bonds maturing in two or less years, proceeds from those maturing bonds would get reinvested in new bonds with lower coupons, which over time would reduce the running yield on the fund. However, that risk is the same with short dated Treasuries, and at least we earn a credit spread to Treasuries.

That credit spread blowing out is also a risk factor. A wider credit spread, would be negative for price performance of the ETF, but it would also reduce the reinvestment risk from any Fed cut. That's because when bonds mature and funds get reinvested, a higher credit spread would, under the same Treasury yields, provide for higher coupons on those newly issued bonds being bought.

In my view, these risks, while real and valid in fixed income, are much less in terms of return magnitude than a prolonged bear market in the stock market would bring about, and the IGSB ETF's defensive characteristics - the very low equity beta of 0.14 - more than compensate for the traditional fixed income risks we would face holding this fund.

ETF Grades from Seeking Alpha

IGSB gets a Buy rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings. In terms of SA's ETF Grades, IGSB also scores very well: A+ for Expenses, Risk and Liquidity, A- for Momentum and C+ for Dividends. For me, Expenses and Liquidity are the key here, as for any ETF investment. We know pay-outs will be less than higher risk products. The fund has traded between $50 a share and $52 a share for the best part of three years. Remember this is a diversifying, risk reducing instrument and our aim is purely coupon clipping to achieve about 5% per year for the next two or three years. When you are ready to get back to increase exposure to equities, you can sell this ETF, having probably achieved a small but low-risk positive total return in the meanwhile. That's why the A+ grade for Risk is important.

Comparison with other fixed income ETFs

I have recently written about three other low cost, liquid fixed income ETFs. They are the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), the iShares 5-10 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGIB) and the iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGLB).

Let's compare the main risk factors, with YTM meaning Yield to Maturity.

IGSB: Equity Beta 0.14. Effective Duration 2.59 yrs. Average YTM 4.98%

AGG: Equity Beta 0.31. Effective Duration 5.78 yrs. Average YTM 4.81%

IGIB: Equity Beta 0.38. Effective Duration 5.93 yrs. Average YTM 5.55%

IGLB: Equity Beta 0.70. Effective Duration 11.88 yrs. Average YTM 6.13%

All four fixed income ETFs are fully invested in investment grade securities, the main difference is the effective duration which drives risk and return. AGG, because it holds a large percentage in government securities, has a relatively lower yield to maturity.

IGSB, IGIB and IGLB are very similar in make up in that they are highly diversified low-cost portfolios of U.S. investment grade corporate bonds, with the key differentiator of where on the yield curve they are invested.

You can read my articles on AGG, IGIB and IGLB on Seeking Alpha here, here and here.

Conclusion

I like all four ETFs because they are all efficiently designed, transparent and low cost and liquid. I can't say which one is best for you because it depends on your risk appetite. For me personally, with the current economic outlook, I see more value in IGSB due to its very low equity beta. Yes, you give up some yield, but in what you get in return is an ETF, from the four covered here, with the best portfolio diversification characteristics and therefore the highest risk reduction properties. And that's what I'm looking for right now.

data.title: Aristotle International Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
(All MSCI index returns are shown net and in U.S. dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Markets Review

Global equity markets started the year on a modestly negative note, with the MSCI ACWI Index returning ‑1.32% for the first quarter. In contrast, global fixed income gained ground, as the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.64%. Value stocks outpaced growth over the quarter, with the MSCI ACWI Value Index outperforming the MSCI ACWI Growth Index by 11.59%.

Outside the U.S., international equities made strong gains versus their American counterparts. The MSCI EAFE Index rose 6.86% during the first quarter, while the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index climbed 5.23%. Within the MSCI EAFE Index, Europe & Middle East and the U.K. were the strongest performers, while Asia increased the least. On a sector basis, nine out of the eleven sectors within the MSCI EAFE Index posted positive returns, with Energy, Financials and Utilities generating the largest gains. Conversely, Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary and Real Estate performed the worst.

Inflation trends remained broadly stable across developed markets. The U.S., U.K., and EU all reported annual inflation below 3%, giving central banks greater flexibility. The Federal Reserve held rates steady, while the European Central Bank and Bank of England continued to ease monetary policy in response to softer growth signals. In Asia, China’s economy recorded 5% GDP growth in 2024, showing tentative signs of recovery, while the Bank of Japan raised interest rates for the third time since ending its negative interest rate policy in March 2024 on expectations of sustained 2% inflation.

Trade policy re-emerged as a market concern during the quarter. In his first months back in office, President Trump announced a new wave of tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico and China, with additional warnings directed at the EU and other trading partners over what were termed “imbalanced trade arrangements.” The targeted industries—autos, steel and aluminum—reflected a focus on reshoring and industrial policy. While the long-term impact is still unfolding, the renewed trade uncertainty prompted central banks to lower growth forecasts and adopt a more cautious tone on future rate moves.

In Europe, Germany’s economy remained under pressure after contracting for a second consecutive year in 2024—the first two-year contraction since 2003. In response, Chancellor-designate Friedrich Merz proposed sweeping fiscal reforms, including an overhaul of the constitutional debt brake, a €500 billion infrastructure fund and increased defense spending. While potentially supportive of medium-term growth, these initiatives have raised concerns around inflation and fiscal discipline, adding complexity to the European Central Bank’s policy path.

On the geopolitical front, a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in January unraveled by March amid disagreements over hostage releases. In Ukraine, a military stalemate in the east opened the door for ceasefire negotiations brokered by the U.S., with discussions reportedly tied to a potential minerals agreement to offset financial aid.

Finally, the artificial intelligence (AI) theme continued to influence market narratives. Chinese startup DeepSeek gained international attention after launching a low-cost rival to leading generative AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic and Google. The move not only intensified competition but raised new questions about the durability and configuration of global AI supply chains. Notably, DeepSeek’s approach—achieving comparable performance at a fraction of the cost—has challenged assumptions around the infrastructure demands of generative AI, potentially reducing the need for GPUs, energy-intensive data centers and the broader hardware stack the market had expected would underpin AI growth.

Performance and Attribution Summary

For the first quarter of 2025, Aristotle Capital’s International Equity Composite posted a total return of 3.62% gross of fees (3.50% net of fees), underperforming the MSCI EAFE Index, which returned 6.86%, and the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index, which returned 5.23%. Please refer to the table below for detailed performance.

Performance (%) 1Q25 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception*
International Equity Composite (gross) 3.62 6.09 4.97 12.10 6.34 5.80
International Equity Composite (net) 3.50 5.60 4.49 11.57 5.84 5.30
MSCI EAFE Index (net) 6.86 4.88 6.05 11.77 5.40 3.18
MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (net) 5.23 6.09 4.48 10.92 4.98 2.80

From a sector perspective, the portfolio’s underperformance relative to the MSCI EAFE Index can be attributed to both security selection and allocation effects. Security selection in Financials, Energy and Industrials detracted the most from the portfolio’s relative performance. Conversely, security selection in Health Care, Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology contributed to relative returns.

Regionally, both allocation effects and security selection were responsible for the portfolio’s underperformance. Exposure to Canada and security selection in the U.K. detracted the most from relative performance, while security selection and an underweight in Asia contributed.

Contributors and Detractors for 1Q 2025

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Munich Reinsurance Cameco (CCJ)
Nemetschek (OTCPK:NEMTF)(OTCPK:NEMKY) Brookfield
Sony (SONY)(OTCPK:SNEJF) Accenture (ACN)
Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY) Ashtead Group (OTCPK:ASHTF)(OTCPK:ASHTY)
Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY)(OTCQX:RHHBF)(OTCPK:RHHVF) Diageo (DEO)(OTCPK:DGEAF)

Cameco, one of the world’s largest uranium producers, was a primary detractor during the quarter. Shares of the Canada-based company declined following President Trump’s 10% tariff on Canadian energy exports to the U.S., where Cameco is a major uranium supplier. However, we believe these tariff concerns are overstated given the inelastic nature of uranium demand and the lack of substitutes. Any incremental costs would be absorbed by utilities under existing contract structures. While the company’s stock price may have followed the decline in uranium spot prices during the period, Cameco’s business is largely insulated from short-term price swings due to its extensive use of long-term contracts rather than spot-market sales. Moreover, the company’s tier-one assets in politically stable jurisdictions, operational track record and ability to flex production—particularly at its MacArthur River and Key Lake mines—further strengthen its competitive advantage. We believe Cameco remains exceptionally well-positioned to benefit as governments around the world increasingly turn to nuclear power as a clean, secure and scalable source of energy.

Accenture, the global IT services and consulting firm, was one of the largest detractors during the period. The company reported revenue at the top end of its guided range, supported by solid booking, particularly in large-scale transformational projects from major corporate clients. Despite these results, shares declined as investor sentiment was impacted by continued client caution amid heightened global uncertainty, including concerns around tariffs and consumer sentiment, as well as the U.S. administration’s initiative to streamline federal operations, which could result in canceled or delayed government contracts. We believe Accenture is well-positioned to support the federal government’s efficiency goals through its expertise and proven track record in delivering innovative, cost-effective solutions. Accenture has also continued to see traction in emerging areas such as generative AI, securing $1.4 billion in new bookings and generating approximately $600 million in related revenue during the quarter. Short-term fluctuations in consulting demand are not unusual, and we remain confident that Accenture’s global scale and deep expertise make it well-positioned to continue to provide solutions and deepen its partnerships with many of the world’s largest companies as they continue to implement increasingly sophisticated technologies.

Munich Re, the world’s largest reinsurance company, was a leading contributor for the quarter. The company delivered strong results despite absorbing €1.2 billion in claims from January’s California wildfires, a testament to the reinsurer’s prudent risk management. Demonstrating confidence in its ongoing profitability and robust capital position, Munich Re also raised its dividend by more than 30% and announced an expanded €2 billion share buyback program. Favorable market pricing and disciplined underwriting have continued to support profitability, even amid elevated volatility in capital markets and global catastrophe losses. Additionally, Munich Re’s experienced leadership team has placed an unusual emphasis on innovation for a reinsurer of its size, investing meaningfully in R&D and technology. For example, the company has identified, launched or already implemented over 300 AI use cases that aim to increase efficiency and enhance its competitive edge. We expect these initiatives to further support Munich Re’s continued market share gains across specialty lines, including cybersecurity, and in rapidly growing Asian markets where insurance penetration remains relatively low.

Sony, the global leader in video games, image sensors, music and movies, was a top contributor for the period. The company delivered strong quarterly results, driven primarily by its gaming and music businesses, and announced a new executive leadership structure. In gaming, Sony reported a record-high 129 million monthly active users, a 20% year-over-year increase in PlayStation Plus revenue and an expanding user base, as 40% of new PS5 console buyers were new to the platform. The Music segment also continued to benefit from global streaming tailwinds, delivering double-digit profit growth. In a significant leadership transition, Sony announced that, effective April 1, 2025, Hiroki Totoki, currently COO and CFO, would succeed Kenichiro Yoshida as CEO. Our original investment in Sony was grounded in the strategic transformation led by Yoshida-san, where Totoki-san was an instrumental partner in driving Sony’s pivot away from commoditized businesses whilespearheading investments in content IP and semiconductors. Looking ahead, we continue to see opportunity for Sony to capitalize on its unique position as both a content creator and platform owner. The company’s ability to integrate gaming, music, anime and film and leverage IP across platforms (e.g., Crunchyroll and its recent partnership with Kadokawa) should position it well for long-term value creation.

Recent Portfolio Activity

Buys Sells
Fast Retailing (OTCPK:FRCOY)(OTCPK:FRCOF) Magna International (MGA)

During the quarter, we sold our position in Magna International and invested in Fast Retailing.

We first invested in Magna International, a Canada-based global auto parts, systems and assembly company, in the fourth quarter of 2019. The company, in our opinion, has a unique capability of supplying parts for an increasingly electrified and autonomous fleet of vehicles. This includes Magna’s specialty in lightweighting vehicles—a necessity for heavy electric cars—as well as its years of investment in self-driving technologies. In addition, with leading market share positions in many of its core markets and products, we believe Magna remains well-positioned to benefit as content-per-vehicle increases and automotive parts and systems become more complex. Though the company continues to meet our Quality and Valuation criteria, we have diminished confidence in its Catalysts. As such, we exited our position in Magna to fund the purchase of Fast Retailing, which we view as a more optimal investment.

Fast Retailing Co., Ltd

Founded in 1984 and headquartered in Japan, Fast Retailing is one of the world’s largest apparel companies. Best known for its flagship brand UNIQLO, the company has over 3,500 stores worldwide, generating approximately 40% of sales in Japan, 20% in China and the remainder across the U.S., Europe and other Asian markets. UNIQLO (originally “Unique Clothing Warehouse”) accounts for roughly 85% of sales, with the remaining 15% coming from brands such as GU, Theory and others.

Fast Retailing is often grouped with “fast fashion” competitors such as Zara (owned by Inditex) and H&M. However, Fast Retailing’s strategy is fundamentally different. While fast fashion brands focus on rapidly turning over the latest trends—often at the expense of quality—Fast Retailing emphasizes timeless, high-quality apparel at accessible prices. Its LifeWear philosophy prioritizes functionality, comfort and durability, with innovations such as HeatTech for warmth and AIRism for breathability making its clothes essential wardrobe staples rather than disposable fashion. This focus on innovation, quality and affordability has helped Fast Retailing build a globally recognized brand with a history of profitable expansion in and outside Japan.

While this is our first time investing in Fast Retailing, we have followed the company for over a decade, having previously invested in Toray Industries, which produces many of the proprietary fabrics that underpin UNIQLO’s appeal. Given its long-term growth potential and operational strengths, we believe now is the right time to invest.

High-Quality Business

Some of the quality characteristics we have identified for Fast Retailing include:

  • Economies of scale achieved with a strategy that focuses on functionality, quality and value (rather than rapidly changing fashion trends) contribute to above peer profitability;
  • Globally recognized brand with a history of profitable expansion within Japan and internationally;
  • Technology-driven innovation in materials, inventory, logistics and supply chain management; and
  • Long-tenured management team with unusually high insider ownership.

Attractive Valuation

Based on our estimates, shares of the company are attractively valued. We believe continued overseas expansion, as well as other catalysts (see below), will likely lead to higher normalized margins and FREE cash flow than currently appreciated by the market.

Catalysts

Catalysts we have identified for Fast Retailing, which we believe will cause its stock price to appreciate over our three- to five-year investment horizon, include:

  • Continued overseas expansion should drive higher normalized revenue and profitability;
  • Operational improvements in China, including better inventory management and an optimized store footprint, are expected to enhance margins; and
  • Improved performance at GU brand in Asia, as well as expansion into the U.S.

Conclusion

As we progress through the early stages of 2025, the convergence of persistent macroeconomic forces with the renewed presence of trade conflicts has added to the uncertainty facing equity markets. While there certainly was no shortage of headlines during the quarter, we believe trying to time the market or to predict the impact of these developments is a futile task. Instead, our focus remains on evaluating whether these events are truly analyzable, materially differentiated and meaningful to long-term investors—or simply noise that fuels short-term speculation.

At Aristotle Capital, we do not aim to capture short-term gains by “trading” portfolios based on the news of the day. Rather, we remain committed to identifying companies we believe exhibit high-quality characteristics and the resilience to perform across full market cycles. In our experience, breaking news is often fleeting and not quite as impactful as many market participants believe it to be. On the contrary, during periods of economic uncertainty high-quality companies are oftentimes able to make decisions that result in market share gains, thus, potentially, increasing their longer-term intrinsic worth. As such, we will continue to study the microeconomic decisions of individual businesses rather than attempt to predict macroeconomic events or outcomes.

data.title: Powell Industries: Valuation At Multi-Year Lows (Upgrade To Buy)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Powell Industries (NASDAQ:POWL) is a key company in trends needed for reliable electrical power systems. Since my previous coverage, the stock has fallen 20% and the valuation has adjusted considerably. However, there have been no fundamental changes and the company is not as at risk from tariffs or the cancellation of data center projects, hence my upgrade to Buy.

Data center projects weaken

In February, I covered Powell's Q1 2025 earnings, detailing the business's fundamentals. Some topics, such as the opportunity within the data center sector, were mentioned. In this regard, there have been updates regarding one of the risks I mentioned: Decreased utilization of data centers.

The Chinese ChatGPT competition stated that it built its model more efficiently, requiring fewer Nvidia GPUs and therefore lower power energy needed (like the one Powell provides).

At the end of March, Microsoft announced that it had abandoned data center projects in the United States and Europe. Subsequently, in early April, the news was confirmed that work on a campus in Jakarta, another in Chicago, and the expansion of its current data center in Wisconsin had been canceled. The stated reason was that current capacity is sufficient to meet anticipated demand, which could be influenced by the Deep Seek announcement I mentioned in my previous coverage.

Since March 25 (the day before the news broke), Powell's shares have fallen 12%. While this hasn't been the only reason, as we'll see later, I think it has a lot to do with it. However, it's worth remembering that only about 10% of sales come from data centers, so while Powell is often considered a data center energy play, that's not quite the case. Yes, this is a rapidly growing segment and could be important in the future, but its largest segment (Oil & Gas) grew 14.40% during Q1 2025, its second-largest segment (petrochemicals) grew 16.98%, and its electric utility segment grew 25.89%. The company is not dependent on data center demand.

Tariff impact

Another of the oft-mentioned issues in recent days, and one that has also played a role in the stock's decline, is Trump's tariffs on all countries. In this case, it's worth starting by mentioning that more than 80% of Powell's sales come from the United States, so the company is not as exposed to potential tariff responses that other geographies beyond Canada (another 10% of sales) may face.

Regarding the source of materials, the company stated since February that the products used are typically purchased within the country where they are sold. This "under one roof" philosophy reduces the risk of tariffs to almost zero, and between that and the majority of production and sales within the United States, the company could be considered practically immune to this effect.

The majority of our product costs are sourced domestically in each one of our geographies that we operate in. And in the event that we do encounter tariff cost headwinds, these will be passed along in the form of price through our pricing models.

Michael Metcalf - CFO.

This means the company shouldn't experience a major impact on either sales or margins for the remainder of the year, despite the two aforementioned phenomena that have developed in recent months. While Q1 2025 margins were slightly lower than in previous quarters, this is because the first quarter is typically the weakest, I'm still interested in continuing to monitor margins in the next quarter, which should be reported in late April or early May.

Valuation and risks

The reason I've decided to upgrade to buy is because the company has fallen 21%. Currently, just looking at the historical EV/EBITDA multiple, we see that we could clearly buy at multi-year lows, except for a period in 2020 following the COVID crisis. However, I don't think there's any reason this time that puts the company's sales at risk or that will deeply damage its margins.

There is one point worth mentioning: the price of crude oil has dropped considerably in recent weeks, dropping from $80 to $60. Considering that Oil & Gas is Powell's largest segment, this could reduce the attractiveness for companies in the sector to invest in Powell's electrical systems for new exploration sites.

However, if we zoom out, we can notice that the price is not in an abnormal range either; in fact, it is quite in line with the average price of the last 10 years and the price of $80 was the one above the average. I doubt that a reversion to the mean would cause projects to fall massively; however, I'm not an expert in this industry, so I cannot give a firm opinion and I would recommend you take this information into account.

Assuming sales end slightly lower than Q1 2025 (24.43% year-over-year growth during that quarter) with similar margins, the company would earn around $170 million in net income this year. With a P/E of 15, which would be below the industry average and Powell's usual range, divided by the 12.2 million shares, the shares price would already justify being worth at least 20% more than their current price and the 5-year annual return would be 14%.

Why my upgrade to buy? Because in my previous article, that same return potential was achieved with a P/E ratio of 20, which is the sector average. In this case, if it trades below the sector average, we have a 20% upside, but if reaches its average, the upside would be 60% by FY2025. That's the beauty of buying with a margin of safety, you're not relying on the ideal scenario as being true to make money.

The bottom line

Powell Industries has been dragged down by the negativity in the data center sector and the broader stock market. However, I don't see any imminent downside risk at the moment and the valuation is now much more attractive with higher potential returns without the need for an ideally high P/E multiple. Hence, my upgrade to buy recommendation.

data.title: Conduent At A Crossroads: Will Investors Recognize Earnings Growth? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

For most of the past decade, the share price of business processing company Conduent Incorporated (NASDAQ:CNDT) has been slipping and sliding lower and lower. It peaked in the low $20s in 2018; at the close on April 14, 2025, it closed at $2.12, roughly a tenth of what it was at its highest.

While revenue was declining along with the share price, net income and basic EPS have made significant gains. That’s left a sizeable gap between price and earnings.

Expect that earnings growth to continue this year, by something around 5%. There are multiple positive signs, including lower expenses. But whether the share price will follow remains an open question.

I now rate Conduent a Hold because I’m not sure if the markets will begin seeing the firm as an attractive investment, with its bottom-line improvements.

About Conduent

The firm provides digital business solutions and services to the commercial, government, and transportation sectors. As management described itself in the 10-K for 2024, “Each day, our solutions and services interact in the lives of millions of people in many ways - from safer, more seamless commutes that reduce congestion to streamlined benefits enrollment, digital payments, customer experiences and government healthcare claims.”

It operates globally and at the end of 2024, had about 56,000 associates, almost 40% of them in North America. Others are based in the Asia Pacific, Latin America, the Caribbean, and Europe.

As this slide from its February 2025 Investor Deck shows, the company is well established with clients:

When I wrote about the company in July 2024, I used this headline, “Conduent Selling Subsidiaries To Enable Profitable Growth”. And I noted the company was undergoing a transition with the goal of improving its growth and profitability.

In the fourth quarter and full-year 2024 results, management reported all three deals were completed, bringing in $780 million after taxes. The proceeds helped it cut its debt by half when compared with year-end 2023.

Conduent also completed a $75 million share repurchase initiative, buying back 52 million common shares, which will be a boost to earnings per share. The buy-backs included roughly 38 million from Carl Icahn and associates.

At the close on April 14, shares traded at $2.12, and it had a market cap of $330.13 million.

Competition and competitive advantages

In the 10-K, it reported heavy competition in all areas in which it does business, adding that it is a leader in many categories. Its competitiveness is based on technology, performance, quality, reliability, reputation, price, and customer service.

Named competitors include multinationals such as Accenture plc (ACN), business process outsourcing firms such as ExlService Holdings, Inc. (EXLS), federal government services providers such as Leidos Holdings, Inc. (LDOS), and transportation multinationals such as Thales S.A. (OTCPK:THLEF).

To assess its competitive advantages, we will compare its net income margin and return on equity performance with the competitors named above. First, net margins over the past decade:

Conduent leads the pack on net margin, but it has been far less consistent than the others. At the end of Q3-2023, its margin was minus 16.91%, and it has come back to put itself into contention again. It’s a similar story when we turn to return on equity:

Based on the data in these charts, I believe Conduent has a medium if not wide moat.

Latest earnings

As the two charts above indicate, its net margin and return on equity levelled out in Q4-2024 after five consecutive quarters of outstanding growth. Let’s put them in context with revenue, net income, and basic EPS over the past decade:

Again, we see a levelling after five quarters of rapid growth. But, what I find interesting here is that net income and basic EPS have trended upward, while revenue trends in the opposite direction.

Normally, I would be pleased to see net income or EPS, or both, growing more quickly than revenue. That suggests the company is becoming more efficient and/or effective in its operations. However, we’re looking at a sustained slump in revenue, one that has lasted 10 years and fallen by roughly half, from $6.66 billion in 2015 to $3.36 billion in 2024.

On the Q4 and year-end earnings call, CEO Cliff Skelton explained,

“Look, as you know, our year was characterized by some intense focus on culling the herd, as they say, for several reasons. We believe this portfolio was and perhaps continues to be too wide, resulting in cost in the center that's too high in a scenario where certain underperforming assets can drag the whole down. That's notwithstanding the fact that many growth companies typically have more scale and depth, and we're progressing towards both. The good news is that we were able to divest several assets at good multiples, allowing us to pay down the bulk of our debt and buy out the Icahn Group, thus streamlining both our strategy and our game plan.”

He added that the sees more opportunities to rationalize the portfolio, “We have assets that without significant investment are a drag to EBITDA, which we will disposition. And we have assets that have scarcity value on the outside but not have the characteristics we think are crucial to our growth and our cash flow imperatives.”

Revenue has been slipping because Conduent has been slimming its portfolio, getting rid of businesses that were not making a bottom-line contribution. I presume that this wasn’t just a problem in 2024, but a decade-long problem.

Those 2024 divestitures allowed it to cut its debt by roughly half, and thus reduce interest expenses, which leads to higher net income and earnings. This transition could be transformational.

With that context, let’s review specifics from the Q4 and full-year earnings report:

Looking at the full-year results, we see most metrics slipped again, including revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow. But we see improvement in net income and EPS, mainly because of the divestitures and a goodwill impairment in 2023.

2024 also saw several initiatives that should help grow revenue and earnings in the future. In addition, it also won a number of awards last year, including the Newsweek Top 100 Most Loved Workplaces. Awards such as these tell us employees are motivated and productive.

It does not pay a dividend.

Conduent’s growth prospects

The earnings report also provided a full-year 2025 outlook. It is forecasting adjusted revenue of $3.200 billion - $3.30 billion, compared to FY 2024’s actual result of $3.176 billion. If Conduent hits this target, it would be the first revenue growth in a decade, a significant milestone.

It projects adjusted EBITDA of about 8.0% by year’s end, as compared to 3.9% in 2024. That would involve a doubling of the amount and percentage performance, again an important milestone if it’s achieved.

Adjusted free cash flow is expected to reach $60 to $80 million by the end of this year, compared with minus $59 million in 2024. Another big reversal, making more funds available for shareholder rewards, as well as organic and acquired growth.

Interest expense is forecast to be about $38 million by the end of the year, compared with $75 million in 2024. That, of course, reflects the reduction of debt from divestitures and will be an important contributor to free cash flow.

Restructuring costs also will be down, by about two-thirds, from $46 million in 2024 to about $15 million this year. If this target is reached, that’s another $31 million available for constructive initiatives.

And CapEx will grow, from $73 million in 2024 to about $80 million in 2025. Higher CapEx usually leads to higher revenue and earnings in the future, another positive signal.

All of this suggests to me that Conduent is about to begin reaping what it has shown in its transitional strategy. The odds are much higher now than they were a year ago that the firm will become a growth stock again, and a profitable one at that.

In this growth context, I’m not sure what to make of the Wall Street analysts’ estimates of normalized EPS:

According to the analysts who made the 2025 and 2026 estimates, normalized earnings will increase substantially in 2026, then slip back again in 2027.

The same analysts have two Down revisions and no Up revisions. Presumably, that reflects the outlook for just 2025 rather than 2025 and 2026. The revisions also are at odds with their two Strong Buy ratings.

A couple of other issues are worth noting. First, CEO Skelton said on the earnings call that changes occurring in federal government operations could be good for Conduent, “we continue to see outsourcing demand as clients continue to emphasize efficiency.”

Second, institutional investors, the so-called “smart money,” are increasing their positions in Conduent. According to Nasdaq data, 104 funds increased their positions by 14.611 million shares, while 76 decreased their holdings by 10.509 million shares. 62 institutions held their previous stakes, representing 95.135 million shares. In total, these investors held 74.31% of shares outstanding.

Both issues, and especially the growth in institutional holdings, are a bullish signal for the future.

Valuation

There hasn’t been much joy in watching Conduent’s share price since September 2018, as shown in this 10-year chart:

The most recent dip, of 10.70% reflects the broader market retreat in response to the tariff announcements. Although its operations are not directly affected by the tariffs—it is a services company, not a products company—its share price couldn’t help but be pulled down as the market declined.

The decline has pushed it into undervalued territory, with a Seeking Alpha valuation grade of A+. I agree with that assessment, but with the caveat that it’s not a satisfactory value stock unless we can expect significant bottom-line growth that will drive up the share price.

I like to set one-year price targets on the basis of EPS, since price will generally follow earnings. However, in Conduent’s case, price and basic earnings divorced in 2019 and have been living separately ever since:

The story is much the same for normalized diluted EPS and the share price. Could the failure of price to follow earnings come from the fact that Conduent’s earnings have been negative for most of the past decade? Have investors simply lost interest and given their attention to other stocks in the industry?

I don’t have answers to these questions, but it appears institutional interest has picked up, given the recent transactions we’ve seen, and as noted, they represent three-quarters of shares outstanding.

Getting back to the one-year target, I will not offer one at this time. In my previous, July 4, 2024 article, I set a target of $4.14 and gave Conduent a Buy rating. At that time, the price was $3.31, and it has since declined, despite earnings rising significantly in the past year.

It is that divergence between earnings and the share price that makes me hesitant to set a price target. When I wrote the July 2024 article, the company's most recent quarterly report, March 2024, saw basic EPS jump from $0.01 to $0.46. I thought that would be a catalyst for the share price, but I was wrong.

Subsequently, EPS more than doubled to $1.09 in Q2-2024. And again, the share price remained relatively flat. When the price does not respond to changes in earnings, I have no idea what it will do in another year.

EPS has fallen since Q2-2024, but I believe Conduent has the potential to become a profitable growth stock in the future, so I am reluctant to rate it a Sell. And so I give it a Hold.

There have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days. The Quant system rates it a Hold, while the Wall Street analysts have a Strong Buy. The Wall Street analysts also have an exceptionally high one-year target: $7.03:

That target seems unrealistic, for at least three reasons. First, it assumes many investors, including some institutional funds, will radically change their pessimistic opinions.

Second, it implies some massive improvement in bottom-line results over the coming year. The company itself forecasts adjusted EBITDA growth of 4.5% to 5.5%, and while adjusted EBITDA and EPS are not the same, that’s probably a reasonable EPS estimate. I think some gains should be expected, but nothing dramatic.

Third, as we’ve seen, Conduent can be pulled down by external issues such as tariffs. As I write this, I don’t see any immediate resolution of that issue, but I do see potential for trade wars and a recession.

On the flip side, it’s much easier to triple the price of a $2-something stock than a $50 or $100 stock.

Risk factors

As noted, there is uncertainty about economic conditions. Conduent may not be directly affected, still, its price could be pulled down by market-wide downdrafts, and its customers may spend less because of the existing uncertainty.

Forecasts of future results, including mine, other analysts, and the company, assume a future that is somewhat similar to the past year or two. At this point, that assumption may no longer be reasonable.

A significant portion of its revenues come from U.S. governments and their agencies. Conduent management believes this may lead to more outsourcing for greater productivity, however, departments and agencies may not have funding for that type of service.

Another important portion of its revenue comes from outsourcing. That stream of income may be disrupted if the governments and companies that use its services decide to handle those business process services internally.

The firm operates in a highly competitive industry. New technologies or business strategies could give competitors an edge that reduces its market share or profitability.

Conclusion

I see many signs that indicate Conduent Incorporated will begin experiencing consistent, profitable growth, rather than losses. The bigger question in my mind is whether the market will begin bidding up its price; it's possible institutional investors may do so, and perhaps pull along individual investors.

While I see the encouraging signs, I’m sticking with a Hold rating for now, waiting to see if quarterly profits keep increasing and how the markets react to that potential growth.

data.title: World Markets Watchlist: April 11, 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Our global markets watchlist tracks nine prominent indexes from economies around the world. The list includes the S&P 500 from the United States, TSX from Canada, the FTSE 100 from England, the DAXK from Germany, the CAC 40 from France, the Nikkei 225 from Japan, the Shanghai from China, the Hang Seng from Hong Kong, and the BSE SENSEX from India. For a look at how some emerging markets across the globe stack up against each other, read our emerging markets update.

Three of the nine indexes on our world watch list have posted gains through April 14, 2025. Hong Kong’s Hang Seng is in the top spot with a year to date gain of 9.14%. Germany’s DAXK is in second with a year to date gain of 4.10% while China's Shanghai is in third with a year to date gain of 0.01%. On the opposite end, Tokyo's Nikkei 225 has posted the largest decline for the year thus far, with a 14.82% loss.

To provide additional context on where these indexes stand relative to their historical peaks, the table below shows each index’s current value, all-time peak, the date of that peak, and how far it is from that record level.

World Indexes and Recent Recessions

Let's start with a very recent chart with the latest recession. We've used February 3, 2020 for our start date (this is the official NBER recession start).

The chart below illustrates the comparative performance of world markets since March 9, 2009. The start date is arbitrary: The S&P 500, TSX, CAC 40 and BSE SENSEX hit their lows on March 9th, the Nikkei 225 on March 10th, the DAXK on March 6th, the FTSE on March 3rd, the Shanghai Composite on November 4, 2008, and the Hang Seng even earlier on October 27, 2008.

However, by aligning on the same day and using a log-scale vertical axis, we get an excellent visualization of the relative performance. I've indexed each of the eight to 800 on the March 9th start date. The callout in the upper left corner shows the percent change from the start date to the latest weekly close.

Here is the same visualization, this time starting on October 9, 2007, a previous closing high for the S&P 500. This date is also approximately the mid-point of the range of market peaks, which started on June 1st for the CAC 40 and ended on January 8, 2008 for the SENSEX.

For a longer look at the relative performance, our final chart starts at the turn of the century, again indexing each at 800 for the start date.

Examples of single country ETFs:

  • WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ)
  • WisdomTree Europe Hedged Equity Fund (HEDJ)
  • KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB)
  • iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA)
  • iShares MSCI Hong Kong ETF (EWH)
  • iShares MSCI Canada ETF (EWC)
  • SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)

Note: I track Germany's DAXK a price-only index, instead of the more familiar DAX index (which includes dividends), for consistency with the other indexes, which do not include dividends.

Original Post
