data.title: ARGT: Lifting Of Currency Controls Paves The Way For The Argentine Economic Miracle
data.description:
Finally, after several months of negotiations, Argentine Economy Minister Luis Caputo announced this Friday a long-awaited agreement between Argentina and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) for an amount of $20 billion, of which approximately $12 billion will arrive as early as next week for the Argentine government to use freely. This far exceeded the consensus of analysts, who estimated that the initial IMF disbursement could be between $8 billion and $10 billion.

With the IMF agreement under its belt and the possibility of obtaining other important monetary support, the Milei government now intends to cancel the outstanding debt of the Central Bank (BCRA) that continued to generate the issuance of pesos through interest payments, as well as the lifting, starting this Monday, of the exchange controls that were still in force in the country since 2019 (cepo).

After a stellar year, Argentine stocks are under strong pressure in the first months of 2025 amid a tariff war between the United States and China that has plunged global financial markets into uncertainty, severely impacting valuations. So far this year, the Argentine equity and ADR ETF, Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (NYSEARCA:ARGT), has seen a significant correction in search of new growth catalysts and as a result of profit-taking by investors.

Despite this, the fundamentals of the Argentine economy remain solid, now marking a very important step in the implementation of the pro-market agenda of the government led by President Javier Milei. But let me first explain what's going on in Argentina and why this will be relevant in the coming years.

Macro Context

Under the exchange control regime officially implemented in 2011, during the administration of Cristina Fernández de Kirchner, with a brief interruption between 2015 and 2019 during Mauricio Macri's term, the exchange controls acted as a restriction on the free availability of dollars in the economy by the population, limiting the amount of U.S. dollars a person could purchase on the regulated official market to $200 per month.

This made both savings and transactions in this currency extremely difficult, fostering the proliferation of a black market for the sale of dollars and creating an exchange rate gap that demonstrated the price distortions prevailing in the economy. In turn, companies wishing to import from abroad or distribute profits and dividends in dollars to their foreign shareholders had to request prior authorization from the State to sell them dollars. Under this restriction, foreign companies wishing to invest in the country could not compensate their shareholders abroad with their returns, making Argentina a much less attractive investment destination.

Following the change in the exchange rate regime, the dollar will now move to a free-floating system between moving bands, abandoning the current 1% crawling peg that set the pace of depreciation of the peso against the U.S. dollar. From now on, the market itself will determine the equilibrium exchange rate through the mechanism of supply and demand for dollars by economic agents, while the dollar exchange rate will be unified, providing greater transparency to the market.

In my last article on Banco Galicia, I mentioned the possibility of this agreement being signed in April and addressed the opportunities that the lifting of the exchange rate restrictions would open up for Argentina with the influx of foreign capital into projects like Vaca Muerta and the country's real estate sector.

This new milestone in President Javier Milei's administration confirms the signal the markets had been hoping for and something many companies were expecting to send a new wave of investments to the country.

In words of Minister Caputo himself:

Ending the exchange rate controls within this macroeconomic context of fiscal and monetary policy will inevitably lead to the influx of investments that are currently pending until Argentina begins this new phase by ending these exchange rate restrictions. These investments will generate greater growth, which in turn will generate a larger surplus that will favor further tax cuts, more jobs, and higher wages.

After reorganizing public accounts based on a sharp 30% cut in public spending since the start of its administration, the Argentine government finally managed to eliminate a chronic fiscal deficit that the country had been carrying for decades, which was key to regaining the trust of the IMF technical staff. This was also achieved with economic growth, a recovery in wages, and a sharp drop in consumer prices for goods and services, which by the end of 2023 were heading towards hyperinflation.

It's also worth mentioning that one of the main drawbacks of the new measures announced by the Argentine government is some limitations on the withdrawal of dividends from previous years by foreign investors. Although companies will be able to freely buy and sell dollars from next week, they will be able to transfer dividends to their shareholders abroad only for those earnings accrued from 2025 onward. Meanwhile, for earnings prior to this year, companies will be able to choose to use their pesos to purchase a special bond issued by the Central Bank (Bopreal) that pays dollars at maturity. After that, these funds will be freely available to be sent abroad as dividends.

Mattress of Dollars

However, Argentina's macroeconomic stabilization program is not limited to the IMF and will have additional international support. In addition to the $12 billion of immediate free availability under the agreement with the IMF, Argentina is in advanced negotiations to receive another $6.1 billion from other international organizations and banks, in addition to a new repo line of credit for up to $2 billion and the renewal of the swap contract with China for up to $5 billion.

Thus, the Argentine president stated that by May, the BCRA's gross reserves are expected to be around $50 billion, of which approximately $28 billion will be available this year. This does not yet include the influx of dollars the country will receive as a result of the harvest season and the arrival of new investments, of which approximately $12 billion is already in projects covered by the new tax incentive regime (RIGI), mainly in the energy and mining sectors.

It's worth adding that this is happening amid a fiscal surplus and having effectively eliminated the unbacked issuance of pesos that was generating inflation. The Argentine central bank now has a strengthened financial and liquidity position to prevent a depreciation of the peso, conditions that didn't exist under previous programs. In fact, the BCRA itself could currently withdraw the country's entire monetary base in pesos (~AR$48T) in exchange for dollars, completely dollarizing the economy with freely available reserves.

The IMF staff projects annualized GDP growth of 5.5% for 2025 (raising its previous forecast of 5%) after a decade of economic stagnation during which the country experienced anemic levels of investment, causing factor productivity to collapse. The organization now forecasts very positive growth for the Argentine economy, driven by solid domestic demand and a carryover effect from stronger growth recorded during the second half of 2024. It also notes that growth in the medium term is expected to converge to around 3% as the stability and pro-market reforms implemented today bear fruit.

Exposure to Argentine Equity

With no growth catalysts in sight to revitalize the upward trend and after being the best ETF for the country globally in 2024, the ARGT has fallen 4% so far this year, the result of profit-taking after a meteoric rise last year and dragged down by the uncertainty prevailing in the markets during the first months of this year. However, despite the recent extreme volatility, the country's macro fundamentals remain very solid, to which is now added a potential catalyst for Argentine stocks.

ARGT has a fairly high exposure to MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI), strongly correlated with the U.S. consumer discretionary sector, which has recently been severely punished by investors amid U.S.-international trade tensions. Furthermore, excluding MELI, we see significant participation in energy and financial companies, which I consider more attractive in this context. Particularly, those with exposure to the Vaca Muerta deposits and projects under development in that region of the country have the best medium- and long-term prospects. For their part, I believe Argentine banks will be among those benefiting from the arrival of investment to the country, much of which will be channeled through the local financial system.

That said, I consider ARGT a good option for gaining general exposure to the Argentine market without being overly selective, also boasting acceptable liquidity and nearly $1 billion in assets under management. On Friday, following the news of the government's lifting of foreign exchange controls, ARGT rose 5.91% after hours, closing an extremely volatile week with a strong 14% rally. However, the ETF's shares are still significantly lagging its regional peers so far this year.

Risks

The possibility that the new monetary regime could trigger a strong depreciation of the peso against the dollar early in the process represents one of the main risks, affecting the inflation levels recorded in recent months. March inflation rose 3.7% month-on-month, above the reading from previous months and an expected 2.6%, driven primarily by an increase in education and food prices. Confirmation of the upward reversal in the price index could once again generate distrust among agents regarding the feasibility of the government's monetary policy objectives and slow macroeconomic progress just months before the country's legislative elections.

Regarding the international scenario, Trump's measures could also cloud the outlook, as the intensification of trade tensions could affect global demand for commodities, negatively impacting Argentina's growth and its ability to access international capital markets. However, it should be noted that Argentine exports to the U.S. were only $6.5 billion (about 1% of GDP) in 2024, so the direct impact of the 10% tariff imposed by Washington on almost all countries in the world, including Argentina, would be less than 0.1% of GDP.

Conclusion

Despite a recent spike in inflation, the macroeconomic context remains highly favorable to Argentina. The agreement reached with the IMF, enabling the arrival of funds to strengthen the Central Bank's reserves, unlocks a wealth of investments that were pending a resolution. At the same time, the Milei administration demonstrates an unwavering commitment to carrying out its structural reform plan to restore the country's growth and restore the economic well-being it once enjoyed.

However, this does not come without internal and external challenges, which must be taken into account when investing in the Argentine market as a whole. In particular, the volatility in global markets resulting from a trade and tariff war between the United States and China has put stocks in jeopardy, especially Argentine ADRs listed on Wall Street, which have experienced sharp declines in recent weeks.

While I consider the country's fundamentals to be extraordinary, this latter aspect leads me to give ARGT a Hold rating while we wait for trade tensions to dissipate and for greater clarity regarding the trade policies of the warring powers.
